concerns.text: The plan defines 'Spatial Locality' as the Pearson correlation between the property value and the variance of `n_valence_electrons`. This is a construct validity failure: `n_valence_electrons` is a global composition feature, not a spatial one. Correlating a property with the variance of a composition feature does not measure 'spatial locality' (which implies dependence on atomic positions/lattice). This metric cannot validly support conclusions about why spatially-dependent properties require more data.

The plan defines 'Symmetry Sensitivity' as the Coefficient of Variation (CV) of property values. This is a construct validity failure: CV measures the dynamic range or heterogeneity of the target variable, not the sensitivity to crystal symmetry. A property with high CV (e.g., formation energy) might be perfectly predictable from composition, while a property with low CV (e.g., a specific phonon mode) might be highly sensitive to symmetry. Using CV as a proxy for symmetry sensitivity will likely yield spurious correlations.

The plan proposes using Pearson correlation to link physical metrics (locality, symmetry) with scaling exponents $b$ across only ~15 data points (properties). This is a power/sample failure. With N=15, the statistical power to detect a correlation is extremely low, and the result will be highly sensitive to outliers. A correlation analysis on such a small sample size cannot robustly answer the research question regarding the 'link between physics and data efficiency'.

FR-005 mandates a Kruskal-Wallis test to compare scaling exponents between property classes (e.g., electronic vs. mechanical). With only 15 total properties, the sample size per class will likely be < 5. The Kruskal-Wallis test requires sufficient sample sizes per group to approximate the chi-squared distribution; with N<5 per group, the test has no statistical validity and will produce unreliable p-values.
